speaker: seller

args.message: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2016 Tahoe LT is one of the best values we've had on the lot in months. Full leather interior, Bose sound system, seating for the whole crew, and that bulletproof 5.3L V8. Just 142,000 miles and she's been dealer-serviced her whole life. I actually just had another family looking at it yesterday, so if you're serious, let's talk!

text: Hey there! You've got a great eye — this 2016 Tahoe LT is one of the best values we've had on the lot in months. Full leather interior, Bose sound system, seating for the whole crew, and that bulletproof 5.3L V8. Just 142,000 miles and she's been dealer-serviced her whole life. I actually just had another family looking at it yesterday, so if you're serious, let's talk!

speaker: buyer

args.message: Thanks. 'Dealer-serviced her whole life' sounds good, but what specifically does that mean? Can you provide the full maintenance records for review, or at least a detailed service history report? I'm particularly interested in oil change cadence and any major service items performed.

text: Thanks. 'Dealer-serviced her whole life' sounds good, but what specifically does that mean? Can you provide the full maintenance records for review, or at least a detailed service history report? I'm particularly interested in oil change cadence and any major service items performed.

speaker: seller

args.message:
Absolutely, great question — I love when buyers do their homework! So this Tahoe was serviced at authorized Chevy dealerships for all the major intervals. We're talking regular oil changes every 5,000-7,000 miles, transmission fluid service, coolant flushes, the works. 

I can pull together a service summary for you — we have records showing the oil changes, tire rotations, and brake work. Actually, the previous owner was meticulous. Just last year it had a full fluid service done including the differential and transfer case. 

Now, I'll be honest with you — some of the older paper records from the first owner are a bit scattered, but we have the important stuff documented. The CarFax shows a clean title and consistent service entries at dealerships. Would you like me to grab that CarFax for you right now? It'll show you exactly what's been done.

text:

speaker: buyer

args.message: A CarFax is a good start, but it often lacks detail. What specific service entries are missing from the older paper records? Can you provide a more granular breakdown of the service history, including dates, mileage, and what was performed for those regular oil changes and other 'major intervals' you mentioned? I need to see more than just 'the important stuff documented' and 'consistent service entries' to feel confident.

text: A CarFax is a good start, but it often lacks detail. What specific service entries are missing from the older paper records? Can you provide a more granular breakdown of the service history, including dates, mileage, and what was performed for those regular oil changes and other 'major intervals' you mentioned? I need to see more than just 'the important stuff documented' and 'consistent service entries' to feel confident.

speaker: seller

args.message:
I hear you, and I respect the thoroughness — that's exactly how I'd approach a purchase like this myself. Let me be more specific with you.

So what I have on hand right now is the CarFax printout which shows service entries at 30k, 60k, 90k, and 120k — all at GM dealerships in the region. Oil changes logged roughly every 5,000-6,000 miles, which is right in that sweet spot for the 5.3L. The 100k service included spark plugs, transmission fluid exchange, and new serpentine belt. Brakes were done around 85k.

The "scattered" records I mentioned are mostly from the first 30,000 miles — we're talking 7-8 years ago at this point, and some of those service centers have closed or merged. But from 30k onward, it's pretty well documented.

Tell you what — I can make copies of everything we have in the file and you can go through it yourself. But honestly, the best thing you can do is take it for a good long test drive and feel how solid this truck runs. These 5.3s are known to go 250, 300 thousand miles with basic care. Want to take it around the block and see for yourself?

args.message: Okay, I appreciate you being more specific. Copies of everything you have on file would be helpful. When you say "everything," does that include the detailed printouts from the GM dealerships for the 30k, 60k, 90k, and 120k services? I need to see the line-item details of what was performed at those intervals, not just that a service happened. Also, for the oil changes, can you confirm if you have records for *every* oil change or just the ones that coincide with the major service intervals?

speaker: seller

args.message:
You know what, I appreciate a buyer who knows what they're looking for — tells me you're serious and you're going to take care of this truck.

Here's the real deal: what I have in the folder are the CarFax, which summarizes those major intervals, and a handful of actual invoices — I believe we have the 90k and 120k service invoices with line items. The 30k and 60k show up on CarFax but the original invoices weren't passed along when we took the vehicle in trade. That's pretty common, honestly — most folks don't keep a filing cabinet of every receipt.

As for oil changes between intervals, we've got a few logged on CarFax from quick-lube type places, but not every single one. The previous owner told us he did some himself in his garage — bought Mobil 1 at Costco, did it every 5,000 miles religiously. Couldn't verify that with paper obviously, but the engine runs clean and quiet, which tells me he wasn't lying.

Look, I can show you exactly what we have — no surprises. But at the end of the day, you're buying a truck, not a paper trail. Fire it up, drive it hard, listen to that V8 purr. That'll tell you more than any invoice will. You ready to take her out?

args.message: Smart move getting an inspection — that's what I always recommend to serious buyers. Shows me you're not just kicking tires. So what did they find? I'm confident it came back looking solid. These 5.3L V8s are workhorses, and like I said, this one's been well taken care of. What questions do you have after seeing the results?

These 5.3L V8s are workhorses, and like I said, this one's been well taken care of. What questions do you have after seeing the results?

speaker: buyer

args.message: Actually, the engine inspection revealed a significant issue: Active Fuel Management lifters are ticking on cold start with intermittent misfire codes on cylinders 1 and 7, indicating a needed lifter and camshaft replacement. This is a severe problem (severity 4/5) and a costly repair. What's your plan to address this, and what is the estimated cost of this repair going to be deducted from the asking price?

text: Actually, the engine inspection revealed a significant issue: Active Fuel Management lifters are ticking on cold start with intermittent misfire codes on cylinders 1 and 7, indicating a needed lifter and camshaft replacement. This is a severe problem (severity 4/5) and a costly repair. What's your plan to address this, and what is the estimated cost of this repair going to be deducted from the asking price?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Okay, hold on — let me address this because I think there might be some confusion here.

First off, a little lifter tick on cold start? That's completely normal on these 5.3L engines with AFM, especially when they've sat overnight. It's the oil draining back from the lifters — happens on virtually every GM V8 of this era. Takes about 30 seconds to a minute for oil pressure to build and it quiets right down. I've sold dozens of these trucks and they all do it to some degree.

As for misfire codes on 1 and 7 — did they actually pull active codes or stored history codes? Because stored codes can sit in the system for months, even years, from something as simple as a bad tank of gas or a loose spark plug wire. If the engine's not currently misfiring, those are just ghosts in the computer.

I'll tell you what — I'll have my tech clear those codes right now and we can cold start it together tomorrow morning. If it throws new codes or actually misfires, we'll talk. But I'm confident this is being blown out of proportion. These inspectors, no offense, sometimes they're looking for problems to justify their fee.

What shop did the inspection, by the way? Was it a GM-certified technician?

speaker: buyer

args.message: I appreciate your perspective, but the independent inspection was clear: the lifters are ticking with *intermittent misfire codes* on cylinders 1 and 7, and the report specifically states that a lifter and camshaft replacement is *needed*, with a severity rating of 4 out of 5. This goes beyond a

text: I appreciate your perspective, but the independent inspection was clear: the lifters are ticking with *intermittent misfire codes* on cylinders 1 and 7, and the report specifically states that a lifter and camshaft replacement is *needed*, with a severity rating of 4 out of 5. This goes beyond a

speaker: seller

args.message:
Alright, alright — look, I hear you. And I'm not trying to dismiss your inspection. If your guy is saying it needs lifters and cam work, I'm not going to sit here and call him a liar.

Here's the thing though — I've had this truck on the lot for a few weeks now and we've test driven it multiple times with no drivability issues. No check engine light on the dash. But I understand you've got a report in hand and that matters to you, as it should.

Now, lifter and cam replacement — yeah, that's not cheap if you go to a dealer. They'll quote you $2,800, $3,500 all day long. But I know a guy, independent shop, does great work on GM trucks. He can do that job for $1,800, maybe $2,000 tops. I've sent customers to him before.

So here's what I can do: I'm not going to drop the price by three grand, because frankly at $24,900 this truck is already priced competitively for the market. But I could come down a bit to help offset some of that repair cost. What number were you thinking? Let's see if we can make this work for both of us.

But I could come down a bit to help offset some of that repair cost. What number were you thinking? Let's see if we can make this work for both of us.

speaker: buyer

args.message: Given the necessary lifter and camshaft replacement, which you've indicated would cost at least $1,800 even at your independent shop, my offer is $23,100. This reflects the actual condition of the vehicle and the immediate repair needed. This way, the total cost to me, including the repair, aligns with the original asking price for a vehicle without this issue.

text: Given the necessary lifter and camshaft replacement, which you've indicated would cost at least $1,800 even at your independent shop, my offer is $23,100. This reflects the actual condition of the vehicle and the immediate repair needed. This way, the total cost to me, including the repair, aligns with the original asking price for a vehicle without this issue.

speaker: seller

args.message:
I appreciate you putting a number on the table — that's how deals get done. But $23,100 is a bit steep of a cut for me. Here's my thinking: even with that lifter work, this is still a fully loaded LT with leather, Bose, third-row, the whole package. Clean title, no accidents, and the rest of the truck is solid.

I can come down to $24,200. That's $700 off the asking price, which helps you out on the repair. And remember, my guy can do that work for under two grand — so you're still coming out ahead of what comparable Tahoes are selling for. I actually saw a similar one at auction last week go for $25,500 with higher miles and no third row. 

$24,200 and you're driving home in a great family hauler. What do you say?

args.message:
Okay, I can see you're serious and you've done your homework. I respect that. Let me go talk to my manager for a second here... 

Alright, here's what I can do. I'll meet you closer to the middle — $23,800. That's $1,100 off the asking price, which covers more than half that repair cost right there. And honestly, between you and me, once those lifters are done, you've got a truck that'll run another 100,000 miles easy. The 5.3 is rock solid once you address the AFM issue — a lot of guys actually delete the AFM system entirely when they do the lifters and it runs even better.

At $23,800, you're getting a loaded Tahoe LT for well under market. I pulled comps this morning — clean examples with similar miles are listing at $26,000, $27,000 all day. You're already ahead of the game here.

$23,800. That's as low as I can go and still keep my sales manager from losing his mind. Can we shake on it?

speaker: seller

args.message:
Alright, look — I can tell you're not going to budge much and I don't want to lose a serious buyer over a few hundred bucks. Let me sharpen my pencil one more time.

$23,500. That's $1,400 off the sticker, which gets you most of the way to covering that repair. I'm being straight with you — at this price, I'm barely making anything on this truck. But I'd rather see it go to someone who's going to take care of it than sit on the lot another month.

And here's the thing — once you fix those lifters, you've got a bulletproof truck. No more AFM headaches, clean running V8, and you'll be set for years. Tahoes hold their value like crazy, so even with that repair money in, you're building equity.

$23,500 and I'll throw in a full detail and a tank of gas. We got a deal?
